comments: This paper investigates using canonical correlation analysis (CCA) to combine phonotactic (SVD-reduced phone n-gram VSM) and acoustic (BNF-based i-vector) vector space models into a single joint VSM for Arabic dialect identification (DID). The authors describe the feature extraction pipelines, the CCA formulation and SVD-based solution, and report that the CCA-based VSM outperforms each individual VSM; after supervised LDA+WCCN post-processing the acoustic-only and CCA representations perform similarly, and the best result is obtained by concatenating the LDA-processed acoustic and CCA representations (final accuracy reported as 60%). Strengths: the paper addresses a relevant DID problem, uses established and sensible modeling choices (BNF, i-vectors, phone n-grams, CCA), and demonstrates that a feature-space fusion approach can be competitive with model-level fusion while also being readily extensible to use unlabeled data. The writing and system description are generally clear. Weaknesses and concerns: the core idea—applying CCA for multi-view fusion—is straightforward and not novel; experimental evaluation is limited in scope and detail in the provided text. The manuscript notes that some discriminative information is lost by CCA but does not probe this phenomenon; selection of hyperparameters (SVD/k, CCA subspace dimensionality c, and LDA dimensionality) and training/validation methodology are not fully justified. It is also unclear whether results are statistically significant, how robust they are across splits, and how the proposed approach compares to other fusion strategies (e.g., score-level fusion, kernel/Deep CCA, or joint supervised multi-view methods). Suggestions for improvement: include full numeric experimental tables (with error bars and statistical tests), ablation studies varying CCA dimensionality and showing per-class performance changes, a deeper analysis of what information CCA discards (visualizations, correlations), and comparisons to additional baselines such as score fusion and more recent multi-view fusion methods (kernel CCA, Deep CCA). If possible, show experiments using out-of-domain unlabeled data in the CCA framework to demonstrate the claimed advantage. Overall, the paper is a reasonable engineering evaluation of a sensible fusion method for Arabic DID but would benefit from more extensive analysis and baselines to strengthen the claims.
